name: Docker Compose Overview (Conduit default)
description: Default board for the Conduit docker profile. Two narratives in one place: the docker host underneath your compose stack (host metrics from /hostfs, broken down by host.name) and the peer apps you've pointed at the agent via OTLP (RED metrics from the M8 span_metrics connector + logs, broken down by service.name). Use the Host filter to scope to one VM and the Service filter to scope to one peer app.

panels.content:
# Docker Compose Overview

The **Conduit docker profile** ships two telemetry narratives that this board ties together:

| Narrative | Source | Primary key | Panels |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compose host** — the VM / server running your `docker compose up` | `hostmetrics` receiver re-rooted at `/hostfs` ([`internal/profiles/docker/hostmetrics.yaml`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/internal/profiles/docker/hostmetrics.yaml)) | `host.name` | Load, CPU, Memory, Filesystem, Disk I/O, Network |
| **Peer apps** — the services on your compose network sending OTLP to the agent | OTLP from instrumented apps + the M8 `span_metrics` connector ([`internal/expander/expander.go`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/internal/expander/expander.go) §`applyREDView`) | `service.name` | Request rate, P95 latency, Error rate, Log volume, Top log templates |

**Why two narratives?** A V0 Conduit docker install does **not** scrape per-container metrics — there is no `docker_stats` receiver, no cgroups walk. That deliberately keeps the privilege footprint small (no docker socket bind-mount, no `/sys/fs/cgroup` access required). The trade-off: you get host-level resource pressure plus app-level RED, but not per-container CPU / memory breakdowns. The host story is honest about being host-shaped; the per-app story is shaped by what your SDKs emit. Per-container metrics arrive with the optional `docker_stats` profile fragment (post-V0, gated on operator opt-in for the docker socket).

**Required for host panels**: the agent's compose file must bind-mount `/proc`, `/sys`, and `/` to `/hostfs` and run with `pid: host` — see [`deploy/docker/compose-linux-host.yaml`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/deploy/docker/compose-linux-host.yaml).

**Required for peer-app panels**: peer containers send OTLP to `conduit:4317` (gRPC) or `http://conduit:4318` (HTTP) on the docker network. The M8 RED-from-spans connector turns those traces into request / error / duration metrics automatically — no extra instrumentation needed beyond the existing tracer.

For the cross-platform telemetry contract this board satisfies, see [`internal/profiles/PROFILE_SPEC.md`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/internal/profiles/PROFILE_SPEC.md).

_Dashboard generated by Conduit V0 MVP. Stored as a checked-in definition under `dashboards/` in the Conduit repo._

panels.name: Load Average (1m / 5m / 15m) — compose host
panels.description: Three-window load average from system.cpu.load_average.{1m,5m,15m}. Sustained values above core-count = the host underneath your compose stack is overloaded, which will manifest as latency spikes in the peer-apps panels below.

panels.name: CPU Utilization (% non-idle) — compose host
panels.description: AVG of system.cpu.utilization (0..1 fraction) by host.name + state, with state=idle filtered out so the chart shows only user / system / iowait / irq / softirq — i.e. effective CPU load on the host. Sustained user>0.8 + iowait>0.2 with growing peer-app latency below = scale the underlying host.

panels.name: Memory Utilization — compose host
panels.description: AVG of system.memory.utilization by host.name + state. The compose host's memory ceiling is what every peer container shares; running out here surfaces as OOM-killed peers in the docker daemon's events (not on this board — check `docker events` for that).

panels.name: Filesystem Utilization (% used) — compose host
panels.description: MAX of system.filesystem.utilization restricted to block-device-backed mounts (device starts-with /dev/) and state=used. The /var/lib/docker/* mounts are particularly worth watching — image / volume disk pressure manifests as 'no space left on device' errors during pulls and builds. The hostmetrics fragment re-roots at /hostfs so the host's mountpoints are reported, not the agent container's.

panels.name: Disk I/O — compose host
panels.description: SUM of system.disk.io by host.name + device + direction. Honeycomb auto-applies INCREASE to the cumulative counter so values are bytes-per-bucket. Watch for I/O hotspots on the device backing /var/lib/docker — a noisy peer-app's logs to the json-file driver, an unbatched volume write, or a chatty image pull all show up here.

panels.name: Network Throughput — compose host (excluding lo and docker bridges)
panels.description: SUM of system.network.io by host.name + device + direction, excluding loopback and the docker-managed virtual interfaces (docker0, br-*, veth*) that carry intra-compose traffic. The remaining interfaces are the host's actual NICs — what's leaving the box. Operators who DO want to see intra-compose traffic should clone this panel and remove the docker* / veth* / br-* filters.

panels.name: Request Rate by Service
panels.description: RATE_SUM of traces.span.metrics.calls by service.name. Filtered to span.kind=SERVER so we count incoming requests once per service, not once per span (an internal span fan-out wouldn't inflate the chart). Sourced from the M8 span_metrics connector — every peer app sending OTLP to conduit:4317 contributes; no extra instrumentation needed.

panels.name: P95 Latency by Service
panels.description: P95 of traces.span.metrics.duration histogram by service.name (server spans only). The histogram buckets are the documented Conduit defaults (10ms .. 10s, nine boundaries) — see internal/config/types.go #REDDefaultHistogramBuckets. P95 from a histogram is approximate within the bucket boundary; sub-10ms tails will read as 10ms.

panels.name: Error Rate by Service (errors / sec)
panels.description: RATE_SUM of traces.span.metrics.calls filtered to status.code=STATUS_CODE_ERROR, by service.name. Compare against the request-rate panel to derive error percentage; an absolute error rate that's holding steady while request rate grows is healthier than a flat error rate at low traffic.

panels.name: Top Endpoints by Latency (P95, last 1h)
panels.description: P95 of traces.span.metrics.duration broken down by service.name + http.route + http.method. http.route is the templated form (e.g. /users/:id) emitted by the M8 default dimension set, so each row is an endpoint shape rather than one row per concrete URL. Top 25 by P95 descending; endpoints without an http.route attribute (non-HTTP services) are excluded.

panels.name: Log Volume by Service + Severity
panels.description: COUNT of docker-host events broken down by service.name + severity_text. Peer apps emit logs over OTLP; Conduit's transform/logs lifts level/severity from JSON bodies (M9.C) and defaults bare records to INFO so severity filters are usable across SDKs that emit different conventions. Watch for service.name=='unknown_service' rows — those are peer apps whose SDK didn't set service.name; they need a CONDUIT_SERVICE_NAME / OTEL_SERVICE_NAME tweak.

panels.name: Recent Errors by Service (last 1h)
panels.description: COUNT of docker-host events with severity_text matching ERROR / FATAL / WARN, by service.name + normalized_message. The normalized_message column has UUIDs, IPs, key=value values, and 4+ digit numbers masked — so each row is a distinct error template, not a unique log line. Peers that emit JSON logs get richer breakdowns (M9.C lifts level/msg into the structured columns).

panels.name: Top Noisy Peer Services (log volume, last 24h)
panels.description: COUNT of docker-host events grouped by service.name, top 10. Identifies which peer apps are dominating the log pipeline — useful for capacity planning (heavy loggers cost more) and for spotting accidentally-debug-on apps in production.
